a-better-did-resolver: Resolving DID documents today means trusting external RPC providers that can serve tampered data. This post explains why SSI needs a Decentralized Universal Resolver Service (DURS): a method-agnostic, trustless resolver that removes single points of failure from credential verification.
a-blockchain-maximalist-disorder-on-chai: Blockchain maximalism pushes everything on-chain, but verifiable credentials already prove authenticity off-chain with digital signatures alone. This post argues for using the chain sparingly—anchoring only minimal proofs and revocation flags—and walks through the privacy, GDPR, and scalability costs of overpublishing credential data.
ai-social-graphs-and-the-rise-of-agent-s: AI agents are starting to form their own social networks—discovering, trusting, and collaborating with other agents instead of working in isolation. This post explores the architecture, reputation systems, and governance of emerging agent societies, and what changes when agents work with each other, not just for us.
beyond-the-did-resolver: A follow-up to the DURS concept that extends decentralized DID resolution into a full verifiable infrastructure: a custom consensus layer for resolution proofs, tokenomics to reward honest node operators, and composable SSI services that interoperate as open protocols.
blockchain-use-cases-in-the-enterprise-w: A practical tour of where web3 delivers real enterprise value—SSI-based digital twins for supply chains, NFT authenticity certificates, token payments and loyalty programs, real-estate tokenization, smart contracts, IoT security, and cheaper cross-border payments.
building-my-personal-ssi-ecosystem-part-: After existing wallets failed to work with a standards-compliant credential issuer, I built my own self-sovereign identity ecosystem from scratch: the Fortune Cookie issuer (OIDC4VCI 1.0, multiple credential formats) and the Light Wallet iOS app (OIDC4VP 1.0). Part 1 of a build series.
comparing-the-most-popular-decentralized: A side-by-side comparison of four major DID methods—did:ion, did:ethr, did:key, and did:web—covering how each anchors identifiers, their trade-offs in scalability, cost, and decentralization, and how to pick the right one for your use case.
create-and-publish-your-own-did-web: A hands-on tutorial for creating and publishing a did:web decentralized identifier: generating an Ed25519 key pair with SpruceID's didkit, converting a did:key document to did:web, hosting it at /.well-known/did.json, and verifying the result.
dijital-kimlik-yoenetiminin-gelecegi-oez: Dijital kimlik yönetiminin kullanıcı adı-şifreden merkezi veritabanlarına evrimini ve bu sistemlerin güvenlik açıklarını inceliyor. Öz Kimlik (SSI) teknolojisinin gizlilik, kullanıcı kontrolü ve birlikte çalışabilirlik açısından sunduğu çözümleri açıklıyor.
enabling-a-sustainable-future-through-se: Digital Product Passports (DPPs) give every product a digital twin to drive transparency and circular-economy goals. This post explains how self-sovereign identity makes DPPs actually work—through decentralized data control, verifiable credentials, and selective disclosure.
enerji-sistemlerinde-esneklik-nedir: Yenilenebilir enerji kaynaklarının değişken arzı, elektrik şebekelerinde arz-talep dengesini zorlaştırıyor. Bu yazı şebeke esnekliği (grid flexibility) kavramını ve depolama, dinamik fiyatlama, arza bağlı tüketim gibi çözüm yöntemlerini anlatıyor.
evil-web-and-the-consent-trap: Today's consent screens and OAuth delegation are optimized for data extraction, not real user choice—scope inflation, dark patterns, and consent fatigue. This post shows how SSI and DIDs can fix consent with purpose-bound, revocable, selectively-disclosed credentials.
fare-dodging-insurance: Berlin's community-funded insurance pools pay members' transit fare-evasion fines, with similar movements in Sweden and Spain. A look at how citizens revived the cooperative roots of insurance and mutual aid to push back against unaffordable public transport.
how-did-scid-addresses-the-limitations-o: did:scid is a new DID method built on Self-Certifying Identifiers—cryptographically verifiable without any third party. This post covers its three pillars (portability, privacy, format flexibility) and how multi-anchoring lets a single DID resolve across peer-to-peer, web, and blockchain sources.
how-to-add-ssh-public-key-to-server: A short, step-by-step guide to uploading your SSH public key to a remote server's authorized_keys file so you can log in securely without a password.
how-to-install-and-use-mosh-on-a-vps: How to install and use mosh, an SSH replacement that survives flaky mobile networks and changing IPs, on an Ubuntu VPS—covering both client and server setup.
how-to-set-nft-as-ens-avatar: A step-by-step guide to using an ERC-721 or ERC-1155 NFT as the avatar for your ENS domain—setting a primary ENS name, adding the avatar record, signing, and validating it.
how-to-use-cloudflare-email-routing-with: A complete guide to running custom-domain email for free: receiving mail with Cloudflare Email Routing and sending from Gmail over SMTP, including SPF/DKIM/DMARC setup, deliverability testing, and troubleshooting.
introducing-the-qroxy-project: An introduction to qroxy ('QR code' + 'proxy'): a dynamic QR code generator built as a REST API backed by MongoDB, letting you change a QR code's destination without ever reprinting it.
know-your-agent-kya: Know Your Agent (KYA) is a framework that gives AI agents tamper-proof digital identities and verifiable credentials—so we can verify which agents to trust before they act on our behalf. Think of it as a future-proof ID system for autonomous AI.
know-your-agent-kya-in-action-mcp-server: How the Know Your Agent concept becomes real: Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers acting as identity wallets for AI agents—storing verifiable credentials, presenting them to services, and enforcing an owner-agent trust chain with revocation as a kill switch.
light-nodes-light-clients-research: Running a blockchain node usually means a heavy full node. This research post compares full nodes and light nodes (light clients)—their trust trade-offs, resource needs, and use cases—and weighs when light nodes make sense, even on IoT devices.
mac-os-setup-guide: A step-by-step checklist for setting up a new Mac for development—macOS security and Finder tweaks, Homebrew packages, iTerm2, Oh My Zsh, tmux, Neovim, VS Code extensions, and essential apps.
nft-proof-of-authenticity: A deeper look at using NFTs as proof-of-authenticity certificates: how an immutable, one-of-a-kind token minted alongside a physical product can verify provenance, fight counterfeiting, and build consumer trust.
open-id-for-verifiable-credential-issuan: OIDC4VCI 1.0 is now a Final Specification. This post breaks down what the milestone means—protocol architecture, authorization and pre-authorized code flows, credential offers, cryptographic key binding, the security model, and production readiness for issuing verifiable credentials.
open-id-for-verifiable-presentations-oid: OIDC4VP 1.0 has reached Final Specification status. This post explains its protocol architecture, the Digital Credentials Query Language (DCQL), same- and cross-device flows, the security and holder-binding model, and what production-ready credential presentation means for SSI.
raspberry-pi-cloudflare: How to access your Raspberry Pi securely from anywhere using a Cloudflare Tunnel instead of risky port forwarding. Note: a fully updated 2025 version of this guide is also available.
re-publishing-nedir-bu-ethereum: 2017'de Medium'da yayınlanan ve birkaç düzenlemeyle yeniden paylaşılan bir giriş yazısı: blockchain'in ne olduğunu, merkezi ile merkeziyetsiz sistemler arasındaki farkı ve Ethereum'un temellerini anlatıyor.
rise-and-fall-of-the-digital-identity: Centralized identity systems keep failing—Turkey's 100M+ and India's Aadhaar breaches show the cost of putting everyone's data in one place. This post argues self-sovereign identity is the clear alternative for security, privacy, and accessibility.
secure-remote-access-to-your-raspberry-p: A fully updated 2025 guide to secure remote SSH access to your Raspberry Pi with Cloudflare Tunnel and Zero Trust—28 detailed steps covering cloudflared install, named tunnels, Zero Trust policies, short-lived SSH certificates, and troubleshooting.
self-sovereign-identity-glossary: A plain-language glossary of the core SSI building blocks—DIDs, verifiable credentials and presentations, holders, issuers, verifiers, identity wallets, zero-knowledge proofs, selective disclosure, and trust registries.
the-decentralization-utopia: Blockchains promise permissionless decentralization but often deliver concentrated validators, whale-driven governance, and centralized foundations. This post examines where decentralization fails, why it happens, and a blueprint for achieving it for real.
the-hidden-identity-verification-tax: Financial institutions spend $206B a year re-verifying identities that have already been checked dozens of times. This post breaks down the hidden KYC tax and argues for reusable, user-owned verified credentials that could pay you back each time they are used.
the-internet-and-identity: The internet was built without a native identity layer. This post explains the two identity models web2 relies on—the centralized model and the federated model—and why each falls short on portability, privacy, and security.
tuerkiye-nin-veri-sizintisi-krizi-ve-oez: Türkiye'deki büyük veri sızıntılarını (108 milyon kişilik veri, e-Devlet, e-Nabız) inceliyor ve bu felaketlerin Öz Kimlik (SSI) ile—dağıtık veri saklama, seçici paylaşım ve kriptografik doğrulama sayesinde—nasıl önlenebileceğini anlatıyor.
verifiable-presentation-exchange-protoco: A comparative overview of verifiable presentation exchange protocols—DIDComm, CHAPI, VC API, and OIDC4VP—explaining how each shares identity credentials and why OIDC4VP stands out as the superior choice.
why-we-need-a-better-identity-solution-f: Our digital identities are fragmented across databases we neither control nor understand. This post lays out the flaws of today's identity landscape and how self-sovereign identity returns ownership, privacy, and security to users.
you-can-t-spell-identity-without-i: A reflection on what identity really is—rooted in the conscious 'I,' not documents or digital records—and how modern identity management has drifted from that personal center, creating risk in both the physical and digital worlds.
